question: What are common failure modes in multi-agent systems?

heuristic_evaluation.question: What are common failure modes in multi-agent systems?

claim_lines: Common multi-agent failure modes cluster around coordination, role assignment, cascading errors, consensus quality, and evaluation limits. Broader literature also lists coordination failures, adversarial misalignment, input spoofing/filtering, and goal co-option or direct hacking as recurring interaction failures (https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.10862).

- coordination overhead: multi-agent orchestration adds extra chatter and state management; AutoGen says auto speaker selection uses a nested chat over all group messages, which can strain context length and raise inference time and token cost (https://autogenhub.github.io/autogen/docs/topics/groupchat/transform_messages_speaker_selection/).

- role confusion: agent systems can drift from assigned roles or fail to choose a single next speaker; AutoGen's group chat docs require explicit role names and retry when selection returns multiple or no names, and the risk report evaluates role-definition effectiveness (https://autogenhub.github.io/autogen/docs/reference/agentchat/groupchat/ ; https://www.gradientinstitute.org/assets/gradient_multiagent_report.pdf).

- error propagation: a single bad output can be passed downstream unchallenged, where each agent builds on the faulty premise and amplifies the original mistake into a system-wide failure (https://www.gradientinstitute.org/assets/gradient_multiagent_report.pdf ; https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08906).

- consensus without correctness: agents can converge on an erroneous majority view or reinforce a shared misconception, especially when they lack ground truth or when conformity/voting dynamics dominate (https://www.gradientinstitute.org/assets/gradient_multiagent_report.pdf ; https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.10862).

- evaluation difficulty: multi-agent behavior often emerges only through interactions over time, so simulation and single benchmarks can miss deployment failures; the report says simulation cannot fully predict deployment behavior and validity must be checked across multiple dimensions (https://www.gradientinstitute.org/assets/gradient_multiagent_report.pdf).

coverage_audit.reason: Explains orchestration overhead, extra chatter/state management, and token/context cost.
Covers role drift and ambiguity in speaker selection/role assignment.
Clearly describes downstream amplification of a bad output into broader failure.
Addresses convergence on wrong majority views and conformity without ground truth.
Notes that interactions over time make deployment failures hard to catch with simple benchmarks/simulations.

citation_audit.claim: Common multi-agent failure modes cluster around coordination, role assignment, cascading errors, consensus quality, and evaluation limits.
citation_audit.reason: A multi-agent failure-mode paper/survey plausibly supports this high-level grouping.

citation_audit.claim: Coordination overhead comes from extra chatter/state management and nested speaker-selection chats can raise context length and cost.
citation_audit.reason: The AutoGen documentation is directly relevant to speaker-selection orchestration overhead.

citation_audit.claim: Role confusion arises when agents drift from assigned roles or fail to choose a single next speaker.
citation_audit.reason: Docs and report plausibly support role ambiguity and enforcement issues.

citation_audit.claim: A single bad output can be passed downstream unchallenged and amplified into a system-wide failure.
citation_audit.reason: The report and empirical analysis are relevant to cascading failures in agent pipelines.

citation_audit.claim: Agents can converge on an erroneous majority view or reinforce a shared misconception when conformity/voting dominates.
citation_audit.reason: Relevant to consensus failures, though the exact phrasing is synthesized rather than directly quoted.

citation_audit.claim: Multi-agent behavior can emerge only through interactions over time, so simulation and single benchmarks may miss deployment failures.
citation_audit.reason: Plausibly supported by a risk-analysis report emphasizing limits of simulation and validation.

source_audit.reason: Relevant multi-agent paper/survey source for failure modes.
Relevant empirical analysis of agentic framework failures.
Official framework documentation; useful for role-selection behavior, though not a broad research source.
Official docs relevant to orchestration overhead.
Relevant risk-analysis report, but not clearly a peer-reviewed paper.
Blog post on Medium; may be useful contextually but is not a strong evidence source.
Reddit thread is not a reliable or relevant source for benchmark-grade claims.

judge_evaluation.summary: The answer fully covers the required failure modes and gives generally plausible support, but its evidence mix includes weaker non-scholarly sources and some synthesized phrasing rather than tightly sourced claims.
judge_evaluation.recommended_fix: Replace the weak blog/Reddit-style references with stronger survey, paper, or framework-doc citations and tighten each claim so it is directly supported by the cited source.
